questions.question:
Five line segments of equal lengths, PR, PS, QB, QT and RT are used to form a star as shown in the figure above. The value of theta, in degrees, is _____________

A. 36   B. 45   C. 72   D. 108
questions.to_find: the tip angle theta of the five-pointed star formed by five equal line segments

understand.plain: Five equal line segments are arranged to form a five-pointed star (pentagram). The tip angle theta is the sharp angle at each of the five points of the star. In a regular pentagram, the five tips all subtend equal angles. The key insight is that the angles inside the star's inner pentagon and the external tip angles are related through the properties of isosceles triangles formed by the equal segments.

keywords.explain: A five-pointed star formed by connecting every second vertex of a regular pentagon. Each tip is a sharp isosceles triangle.
keywords.example: The star on a US flag is a pentagram.

keywords.explain: A triangle with two equal sides. Here each arm of the star is formed by two equal segments meeting at the tip.
keywords.example: Triangle PRT where PR = RT (equal segments) is isosceles.

keywords.explain: An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles.
keywords.example: If a triangle has tip angle theta and two base angles beta, then 2*beta + theta = 180 deg.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 310' width='300' height='310'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='310' rx='10' class='bg'/><polygon points='150,30 190,148 305,148 211,215 246,333 150,265 54,333 89,215 -5,148 110,148' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5' transform='translate(0,-20) scale(0.88)'/><polygon points='150,22 186,135 296,135 205,198 238,310 150,248 62,310 95,198 4,135 114,135' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='148' y='18' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='275' y='112' text-anchor='start' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='225' y='295' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='70' y='295' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S</text><text x='20' y='112' text-anchor='end' class='lbl ink'>T</text><text x='150' y='44' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>theta</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A five-pointed star (pentagram) with vertices labelled P at top, Q at upper right, R at lower right, S at lower left, T at upper left, and the tip angle theta marked at the top vertex P.

given.aim: Find the tip angle theta of the star at each point

terms.meaning: PR = PS = QB = QT = RT, each segment being a side of the star's arms
terms.example: Since PR = RT, triangle PRT is isosceles with the apex at R.
terms.connects: Equal segments create isosceles triangles at each intersection point of the star.

terms.meaning: The five vertices where the star's lines intersect form a regular pentagon inside the star.
terms.example: In a regular pentagram, the inner pentagon has each interior angle = 108 deg.
terms.connects: The interior angles of the pentagon determine the base angles of the tip triangles.

terms.meaning: The sum of all three angles in any triangle = 180 deg.
terms.example: In the tip triangle at P: theta + base_angle + base_angle = 180 deg.
terms.connects: Knowing the base angles allows calculation of the tip angle theta.

given.plan: Use the property that in a regular pentagram, the inner pentagon has interior angles of 108 deg. Each tip triangle has two base angles equal to 180 - 108 = 72 deg. Then theta = 180 - 2*72 = 36 deg.

steps.title: Interior angle of the regular pentagon
steps.formula_raw: Interior angle = (n-2)*180/n  for n-sided regular polygon
steps.note: The five crossing points of the star form a regular pentagon with each interior angle = 108 deg.

steps.title: Base angles of each tip triangle
steps.formula_raw: Angles on a straight line sum to 180 deg
steps.apply: At each base vertex of a tip triangle, the angle inside the triangle = 180 - 108 = 72 degrees
steps.note: Each tip triangle is isosceles (two equal segment lengths), so both base angles are 72 deg.

steps.title: Compute the tip angle theta
steps.note: This is the well-known result: every tip of a regular pentagram measures 36 deg.

questions.formula_note: All three formulas are standard geometry results; the polygon interior angle formula generalises the triangle (n=3 gives 60 deg) to any n-gon.

questions.question:
During a half-moon phase, the Earth-Moon-Sun form a right triangle. If the Moon-Earth-Sun angle at this half-moon phase is measured to be 89.85 degrees, the ratio of the Earth-Sun and Earth-Moon distances is closest to

A. 328   B. 382   C. 238   C. 253
questions.to_find: the ratio of Earth-Sun distance to Earth-Moon distance when the Moon-Earth-Sun angle is 89.85 degrees

understand.plain: At half-moon, the Sun-Moon-Earth angle is exactly 90 degrees because the Sun illuminates exactly half the Moon's face as seen from Earth. This means Earth, Moon, and Sun form a right-angled triangle with the right angle at the Moon. Knowing the angle at Earth (Moon-Earth-Sun = 89.85 deg), we can use trigonometry to find the ratio of the two distances. The ratio ES/EM equals 1/cos(angle at Earth).

keywords.explain: The Moon appears as a half-disc when the Sun-Moon-Earth angle is exactly 90 deg, meaning the right angle of the triangle is at the Moon.
keywords.example: At first or last quarter moon, we see exactly half the lunar face lit up.

keywords.explain: The angle at the Earth vertex of the Earth-Moon-Sun triangle; it is very close to 90 deg because the Sun is much farther away than the Moon.
keywords.example: 89.85 deg is only 0.15 deg away from a right angle, indicating the Sun is roughly 382 times farther than the Moon.

keywords.explain: In a right triangle, cos(angle) = adjacent/hypotenuse. Here cos(89.85 deg) = Earth-Moon/Earth-Sun.
keywords.example: cos(89.85 deg) = EM/ES, so ES/EM = 1/cos(89.85 deg).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 200' width='340' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='60' cy='130' r='14' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><text x='60' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Earth</text><circle cx='150' cy='60' r='10' fill='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)'/><text x='150' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Moon</text><circle cx='310' cy='125' r='20' fill='#F5C842' stroke='#C9A000' stroke-width='2'/><text x='310' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Sun</text><line x1='60' y1='130' x2='150' y2='60' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='150' y1='60' x2='310' y2='125' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='60' y1='130' x2='310' y2='125' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='90' y='115' class='tag no'>EM</text><text x='185' y='170' class='tag no'>ES</text><rect x='140' y='60' width='12' height='12' fill='none' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='68' y='122' class='sub mut'>89.85 deg</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Right triangle with Earth at bottom left, Moon at top middle (right angle), and Sun at bottom right. The angle at Earth is 89.85 deg, EM is the short side and ES is the long hypotenuse-adjacent.

given.aim: Find ES/EM (ratio of Earth-Sun distance to Earth-Moon distance)

terms.meaning: At half-moon, the Sun-Moon-Earth angle = 90 deg because sunlight hits the Moon perpendicularly relative to the Earth-Moon line.
terms.example: This makes the Earth-Moon-Sun triangle right-angled at M.
terms.connects: Places the hypotenuse as Earth-Sun (ES), with EM and MS as the two legs.

terms.meaning: The Moon-Earth-Sun angle (at vertex E) is given as 89.85 degrees.
terms.example: This tiny deviation from 90 deg tells us the Sun is very far compared to the Moon.
terms.connects: This is the angle we use in the cosine formula: cos(89.85 deg) = EM/ES.

terms.meaning: The sought quantity: how many times farther the Sun is compared to the Moon from Earth.
terms.example: Historically, Aristarchus measured this ratio to be about 19 (far too small), but the true value is about 390.
terms.connects: ES/EM = 1/cos(89.85 deg) = tan(90 - 89.85) = tan(0.15 deg) is wrong direction; use cos directly.

given.plan: In the right triangle at M: cos(angle at E) = EM/ES, so ES/EM = 1/cos(89.85 deg). Compute cos(89.85 deg) = cos(90 - 0.15) = sin(0.15 deg) approx 0.00262, giving ES/EM approx 382.

steps.title: Identify the right triangle geometry
steps.formula_raw: cos(angle) = adjacent/hypotenuse  in a right triangle
steps.apply: Right angle is at Moon. Angle at Earth = 89.85 deg. Adjacent side = EM (Earth-Moon). Hypotenuse = ES (Earth-Sun). Therefore cos(89.85) = EM/ES.
steps.note: The Earth-Sun line is the hypotenuse because it is opposite the right angle at Moon.

steps.title: Rearrange for the required ratio
steps.note: We need to evaluate cos of an angle very close to 90 deg, which is nearly zero, giving a large ratio.

steps.title: Evaluate cos(89.85 deg)
steps.apply: cos(89.85 deg) = cos(90 - 0.15) = sin(0.15 deg). Now sin(0.15 deg) = sin(0.15 * pi/180) approx 0.15 * pi/180 approx 0.002618
steps.note: For very small angles in radians, sin(x) approx x, so sin(0.15 * pi/180) = sin(0.002618 rad) approx 0.002618.

steps.title: Compute the final ratio
steps.note: This matches the closest option B = 382.

questions.formula_note: The cofunction identity cos(90-x) = sin(x) is the key step that converts cos of a near-90-deg angle into a small sine, making the numerical evaluation straightforward.

questions.question:
Seven identical cylindrical chalk-sticks are fitted tightly in a cylindrical container. The figure below shows the arrangement of the chalk-sticks inside the cylinder. The length of the container is equal to the length of the chalk-sticks. The ratio of the occupied space to the empty space of the container is

A. 5/2   B. 7/2   C. 9/2   D. 3
questions.to_find: the ratio of occupied space to empty space inside the cylindrical container holding seven chalk-sticks

understand.plain: Seven identical cylinders are packed in a larger cylinder: one central cylinder surrounded by six others arranged symmetrically. Each small cylinder has radius r. The six outer cylinders touch the central one and also touch the container wall. This hexagonal packing means the container radius R = 3r (one centre radius r plus two outer radii 2r). The total cross-section of seven small circles is 7*pi*r^2 out of the container cross-section pi*(3r)^2 = 9*pi*r^2. The empty cross-section is 2*pi*r^2, giving ratio 7:2.

keywords.explain: One cylinder at the centre surrounded by six cylinders of the same radius. The centre-to-centre distance between adjacent cylinders equals 2r.
keywords.example: Six coins of the same size arranged around one central coin of the same size form this pattern.

keywords.explain: The outer cylinders are tangent to the container wall. Their centres lie at distance 2r from the central axis (r for half the central + r for half the outer). Adding the outer radius r gives R = 2r + r = 3r.
keywords.example: If each chalk-stick has radius 1 cm, the container has radius 3 cm.

keywords.explain: Since all cylinders have the same length, the volume ratio equals the cross-sectional area ratio.
keywords.example: Occupied area = 7*pi*r^2; Total container area = pi*(3r)^2 = 9*pi*r^2; Empty = 2*pi*r^2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 260' width='260' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='260' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='130' cy='130' r='117' fill='none' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='130' cy='130' r='39' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='130' cy='52' r='39' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='197' cy='91' r='39' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='197' cy='169' r='39' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='130' cy='208' r='39' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='63' cy='169' r='39' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='63' cy='91' r='39' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='130' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>r</text><text x='130' y='244' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>R = 3r</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Cross-section view of a large container circle (radius 3r) with one central small circle (radius r) surrounded by six identical small circles, all fitting tightly inside the container.

given.aim: Find the ratio of occupied volume to empty (unoccupied) volume in the container

terms.meaning: All seven chalk-sticks have the same circular cross-section radius r.
terms.example: If r = 1 cm, each chalk-stick's cross-section area = pi cm^2.
terms.connects: The container radius R is expressed in terms of r through the packing geometry.

terms.meaning: With one central stick and six around it, the outer sticks' centres are at distance 2r from the axis, so the container wall is at 2r + r = 3r.
terms.example: For r = 1 cm, the container's cross-section area = pi*(3)^2 = 9*pi cm^2.
terms.connects: This determines the total container cross-section area = 9*pi*r^2.

terms.meaning: Chalk-sticks and container have the same length L, so volume ratios equal cross-sectional area ratios.
terms.connects: Cancels L from both numerator and denominator, leaving a 2D area ratio.

given.plan: Find container radius R = 3r from the hexagonal packing. Compute total container area = 9*pi*r^2, occupied area = 7*pi*r^2, empty area = 2*pi*r^2. The required ratio = 7/2.

steps.title: Determine container radius from packing geometry
steps.formula_raw: R = r + 2r = 3r  (one central circle of radius r surrounded by six of radius r)
steps.apply: Centre of outer circle is 2r from container axis (= r + r, centre-to-centre). Outer circle extends r more to the wall. So R = 2r + r = 3r.
steps.note: This hexagonal arrangement is optimal: exactly six circles of radius r fit around one central circle of radius r.

steps.title: Compute cross-sectional areas
steps.apply: Container area = pi*(3r)^2 = 9*pi*r^2. Occupied area (7 sticks) = 7*pi*r^2. Empty area = 9*pi*r^2 - 7*pi*r^2 = 2*pi*r^2.
steps.note: The pi*r^2 factor cancels when forming the ratio.

steps.title: Compute the required ratio
steps.note: The ratio is 7/2 = 3.5, independent of the actual radius r.

questions.formula_note: The circle area formula pi*r^2 appears in both numerator and denominator and cancels, so only the integer coefficients 7 and 2 (from the container radius R = 3r) matter in the final ratio.

formulas.name: Interior Angle of a Regular Polygon
formulas.plain: Interior angle = (n-2)*180/n  degrees, for a regular n-sided polygon

formulas.name: Supplementary Angles (Angles on a Straight Line)
formulas.plain: Two angles that together form a straight line sum to 180 degrees

formulas.name: Triangle Angle Sum
formulas.plain: The three interior angles of any triangle sum to 180 degrees

formulas.name: Trigonometric Ratios in a Right Triangle
formulas.plain: sin(theta) = opposite/hypotenuse; cos(theta) = adjacent/hypotenuse; tan(theta) = opposite/adjacent

formulas.name: Cofunction Identity
formulas.plain: sin(90 - x) = cos(x)  and  cos(90 - x) = sin(x)  for any angle x in degrees

formulas.name: Hexagonal Circle Packing Radius
formulas.plain: One central circle of radius r surrounded by six circles of radius r fits inside a container of radius R = 3r

formulas.name: Area of a Circle
formulas.plain: Area = pi * r^2  where r is the radius

formulas.name: Ratio of Two Areas
formulas.plain: Ratio = A1 / A2  where A1 and A2 are the two areas being compared
